One of the best people in marketing that I ever had the privilege to work with was Bob King and Phillips. ## [06:05 - 09:06]

amortized over many years and create an income stream from each customer. It's not just a one-time transaction, but the lifetime value of a customer can be very high if, again, we keep that goal in mind that there is a lifetime relationship with a customer and that it's not just a one-time transaction. It's not a one-night stand. It's really a marriage where you're going to be doing good things with and for your customer for many, many years to come. The way Bob King expressed his Bob being involved in a publishing business, selling newsletters, he corrected my thinking by giving me this quote. He said, we are not in the newsletter business. We are in the renewal business because most profits in any business come from the repeat sales and that's true for almost any business. So whenever you're thinking of how to serve your customers, never think one shot. Always think of the long-term relationship. Team direct marketers are happy to break even and just selling something to a customer if on the back end they can look forward to many repeat sales. Probably the first and best marketer to do this was a man named King Gillette. He was just a genius of marketing, a very peculiar eccentric man. He had the brilliant notion. He was a success, quite late in life. He was friends with the head of the Crown Cork and Seal Company or something like that where they made a fortune on bottle caps. Because every bottle, you take the bottle cap off, you toss it away and you have to buy more bottle caps. And so the head of that company said, you know, King, I know you have great ambitions and you haven't been a success yet in life according to your grand ambitions anyway, but you can make a lot of money by thinking about customers in terms of an anuitizing or wealth as an income stream. And so King Gillette thought about this and he was a traveling salesman, King Gillette, and went on a train. Most of the men of that time, this is in the, I guess, the early 1900s, not sure of the date, but they would shave with a long straight razor. And if you're a traveling salesman on a train that's jostling back and forth with a straight razor, then you could do a lot of damage. So there was some razor blades at the time that, but they weren't that sharp. So he got the idea of, let me create a very sharp razor blade, but the brilliance of what he did was he gave away or sold very cheaply, depending on his latest ad campaign, he virtually gave away the razor handle. And so lots of men would love to get a free gift, a razor handle. And of course, the only blades that fit in the razor handle were Gillette blades. And so he created lifetime customers by giving away that razor handle. And that's a brilliant marketing strategy that many of us can use. Mobile phones do the same thing to a certain degree by charging you very little for the mobile phone, but then having a plan where you pay by the month.

## [09:07 - 12:09]

If you've noticed, when we bought our first computer printer many years ago, it was very expensive, it was several thousand dollars. Now the print is very cheap, but when you go to reorder the toner, you see what they're up to. They're up to the same thing as King Gillette, where they make their money on the repeatable sales. You can sell a printer only once, but the toner runs out every so many weeks. So they make it easy for you to get started in their income stream, and then you keep paying income as long as you're using their razor blades or mobile phone or inkjet printer. Apple iPod is a similar thing. The iPod is a great piece of technology. But its real purpose is to sell you the songs for a dollar a piece on the iTunes connection with Apple. That's really where they make their back end money. E-zines are very similar. They're a great vehicle for signing somebody up with a free e-zine, and then that can feed into selling many products later on. So always think of that. Don't think of a one-time opportunity to make money. Always think of how can I convert this contact of the customer at the first time into a continuing, repeating revenue stream. Makes a tremendous difference. So that could lead us very naturally. That's how marketers can make a lot of money. How can copywriters do the same thing? Well, let me give you a few tips of what I've found in my career. And my father really led me to have this philosophy. He did what he loved in life. He was a big, strong Italian man. But his love in life was tending flowers. And he was a gardener, and he just loved doing that. And he got a job with the New York City Park Department that let him work with plants and trees, and working in many of the nice parks around the city. Because he loved working with plants. And he told me when I was in high school, he said, look, we don't have enough money to send you to college if you want to do that. Do what you're gonna have to go at night, which is what I wound up doing. The most important thing, I don't care what you want to do in life, Gary. I'm not gonna give you any big speech about what I expect of you. The only thing that I expect of you is to find work you love, because then you're gonna be happy every day that you go to work. Work will become your play. In fact, Noel Coward probably said it best, where work is more fun than fun when you really love your work. And I consider that my father's greatest gift. He said, don't worry about pleasing me or pleasing anybody else. You find what you want to do in life that you have an aptitude for, and you'll, every day you go to work, that'll be your fun. And you'll automatically become one of the best at it, because if you're always thinking of what you love to do, then you'll put more time in automatically, because it's your joy than people who view it as a dreaded thing that they have to get away from as soon as they can. So I promptly told him that I had every intention to be the shooting god for the New York Knicks. And given my suspect jump shot, he said, well, maybe you want to plan B. And that's when we talked about copywriting, and that's how I got started. Another very good point is to always remember whether you're working for

## [12:09 - 15:10]

yourself or working at a corporation. We are all in business for ourselves. I used to have this philosophy, and it really kept me in good stead. Even when I worked at some very large corporations, a lot of ad agencies and different corporations that I started out working for. I always felt that I really can't take them for granted. They are really my customer. And in fact, if I were in business, I'd have probably several customers, but since I'm in business with this company, I really, I have one dominant customer. When you work for somebody, you have one dominant customer, and you really have to be sure to keep your customer happy. So I would recommend that to everyone, because very often people say, do you think I should go into business for myself? You know, what's your experience? And I say, you are in business for yourself. You're always in business for yourself, because no matter who you work for, there's somebody that you have to make happy with your work. And never forget that or else you'll start taking people for granted. This is a great little tip I learned from the book, The Richest Man in Babylon. At least 10% of all you earn is yours to keep. And that's 10% of your gross income. You should be able to, no matter how little or much you're earning, you should be able to salt away at least 10% of your gross income every year, even when you're young. And if you do that and start letting that work in the miracle of compounding, an IRA program or whatever, you'll be very surprised at how much that money that can grow to. If you can put a salt away 20%, that's really fantastic. But 10% is a bare minimum that anybody who earns any amount of money should, at all, should be putting away for the future. Number four, if you're a freelancer, don't let one client become more than 50% of your income. One of the agencies I work for let this happen. And I saw it coming that they let one very generous, very successful client become more and more and more of their business. And this is very easy to let happen when you're freelancing or in effect a one person business. Because they have a growing client who really starts dominating a business. They want more and more work. And it's very easy for them to crowd at your schedule. And I think I would strongly recommend that you put a limit on that of about 50% of your time. Even if you don't have anything to do in the other 50%, you should invest that time in marketing and getting some other clients to fill up that other 50%. Because if you grow too dependent on one client, if 95% or 100% of your income is from one client, and then that client sells the business or takes to work in-house, you've just gone from 100% income to 0%, and that's a very jolting bump to have to go through. So if you're managing your practice as a freelancer or small business properly, you cannot let any one client be more than 50% of your income, in my view. In your negotiations, I've always tried to treat all clients equally because they talk with one another. Never believe it if a client says, look, this is going to be secret. Give me a good deal, and nobody else will hear about it. The next day, you may well get a call from a competing client saying,

## [15:10 - 18:16]

how come you gave someone such a good deal? You charged us so much more for direct mail package, and now they're getting it for less. People change positions, even if the person is telling the truth that they really are keeping it secret. Sooner or later, if you have inconsistent pricing policy, people will really start to hammer you in their negotiations to make sure they're getting the price, the best price they can. As part of that, always expect your clients to give you the ouch technique, no matter what price you quote. They may say ouch. But the best antidote to that is to really try to hold firm and just figure out what you need at your level of demand in the marketplace to figure out what you need to get the job done and what your goals are. And in any negotiation, what little I know about negotiation, I remember George Meadie, the very tough negotiator for the AFL-CIO, said, look, never sit down at the negotiating table unless you're also willing to get up and walk away. So when you're in negotiations, remember, you don't have to come to an agreement if it's not making you happy from your end. Now, here's a great negotiating tip for clients from somebody with the odd name of George George. I don't know if any of you know him, but he was an executive, maybe he still is. I know I had double-day books. And double-day, for many years, has been based in Garden City, where I live. And I got a call one time from George George, and I was charging like $25,000, my steps, it's the last 25 years, it's $25,000 upfront and with a five cents per piece direct mail royalty. And I was unbending in that, as my clients here will attest. But George George called and said, Gary, gee, we're double-day. We're a book club right in town. I'd like to take you to lunch. Come on over, we're neighbors, just to say hello. So I had nothing to do one afternoon taking it off. And so I went over to see George George. And George said, we'd love you to knock out a couple of these little brochures, a little insert, you could do this virtually in your sleep. The most I can pay you is $400 per piece, to do this little brochure. But you could do it very quickly. And somehow I said yes. It was like, I'm charging $25,000, it was all set to give him the speech. I don't charge anybody less than $25,000. But these little things, they're virtually all done, they were so easy. And I just needed to break every now and then from the really heavyweight jobs. And it was such a pleasure. I said, well, for a couple of hours worked $400, and I'll do it. But that's really such an interesting technique. If you are a client to say to someone, this is what the budget offers, regardless of what their pricing structure is, to say this is what the job offers. I hope you can do it, it's fairly easy if you can just knock it out. And that worked with me, and it was so easy. It would take me an hour or two, and I enjoyed reading about the books that they were offering. So it's not a bad technique when you're negotiating with others. Ted Nicholas did the same thing with media. He would send his ad to different media, and you probably have heard this, if you know about Ted's work, a brilliant space ad marketer, probably the foremost space ad marketer in the last several decades. He would send his ad to the media and say, look, I know you're a card is $8,000 a page.

## [18:16 - 21:20]

I'm a mail order advertiser. I can only afford four. Here's my check. If you can run the ad for $4,000, cash my check and run it. If you can't, please send my check back. And he said it's almost humanly impossible for a magazine space ad salesman to take that check and put it back in the envelope and send it back to him. So more often than not, he would get the ad run at half price. And then he said, oh, by the way, I do need good right-hand side-page position. And then he would give all the rest of the requests that he had for his half price offer. And more often than not, it worked. What about working for competing clients? This is always a ticklish proposition. And I know some of the direct marketers in this room are always confronting this choice. They give an assignment to a copywriter. He or she works on it. And then a competitor calls and has a similar assignment. How is that handled? And I want to get into a discussion after this about ways that clients feel that they can work better with the writers and ways that writers feel that clients can help them more so that both sides can make more money in our marketplace. This is a ticklish question. My feeling always was that because I specialized in the field, I really gained more by working for other similar clients. Because you're exposed to a lot more tests, you're much more involved with the state of what that market is doing. And luckily, I was in demand enough. Because again, if you have enough control packages, you have a lot of leverage in whatever you're negotiating. Because that's what our clients live and die by, is all the control packages. So that buys a lot of tolerance. Having a lot of control packages out there will let your clients bend the rules a little bit if they have to. David Olgavies rules for what he calls successful polygamy, is pretending that each spouse was the only pebble on the beach. So you should do that. If you are working for a competitive client, don't make that obvious and rub it into your other client. That really does start to create some professional jealousy. By all means, keep secrets that are shared with you, confidences. And sometimes clients will test you. Dan Rosenthal, the head of the agency where I worked many years ago, whenever we had a major job in the company, a major job opening, he would interview people from other agencies. And he would try to pry confidential information out of them. And we'd have a nice session in the interview for a while. And then he would say, but you really got to tell me, what is that? I heard about the new campaign they're working on. Give me a little information about that. And the person initially might say, well, that's kind of I should not talk about that. He'd say, oh, come on, what am I going to do with it? I really would like you to tell me. And he would only start getting angry at the job candidate in the process. But this was all a test. If the job candidate held firm and just ethically tried to dodge the question or change the subject or just say, look, I really can't talk about that,

## [21:20 - 24:20]

that person went way up in Dan's estimation. If they tried to win his favor, apparently, by sharing confidential information, they were instantly eliminated from consideration. Now building wealth with royalties, what I've learned about that. I started with royalties. May have been either the first or one of the first copywriters to really try to forge royalty arrangements with clients I was working with. Not so much to make money. I wanted to do it as a defensive measure because more and more and better and better writers were coming into the field just as they were out today. One of the reasons I'm retiring, so many of you are so good. Where I saw that if I could put more time into the research and into the preparation of the advertising, the more time I would put in, the stronger my work would be. And if I could have a much bigger payout if I win, then I'd be able to serve the clients a lot better. So that was really how I started with royalties. And the clients were perfectly comfortable with that. And I was able to then start putting, instead of finishing a job in three or four weeks, I would start putting two months. And then even three months into it because the more I researched, the deeper I would go into the research, the more I would look at what's working, what's not working. I was able to just, I'm one of those people who even in school, I was never like the head of the class just on brains, but I could always figure I could outwork somebody. And if I was persistent and just try to just work a little harder, I would usually win. And that gave me the means to do that because I knew if I put in the time, I'd have the potential payout. So that's how that started really, at least for me. And most of the initial royalty arrangements that I had were with clients with whom I had fee arrangements prior to that. So people over the years have writers, some of them in this room is saying, well, Gary, I'd like to go on royalties too. Can you tell me what your contract like? And I've never really had a contract for royalties because I grew up with the clients who I worked with on fees. And from them, such good people as Phillips and Leder Rodale and Boardroom, people like that you don't have to have a contract with. They shake your hand and their word is their bond. You might want to have just a letter of understanding perhaps to just see what gets counted as a royalty, what doesn't, but that's just for clarification. It's not for legal use later on. Contrary to that, one of the best attorneys I ever spoke with said that any client who you don't trust, even if you have an ironclad contract, people will find some way to wiggle out of the contract. So if you're thinking of going with royalties, the character or the person you're dealing with is the most important factor. If you trust a person and they are a large enough company where there are several people involved with the calculation and payment of royalties, then you can fairly well trust. Because even if they trust that they're going to honor their agreement. Because even at a company, a major mailer, if somebody wanted to cheat you out of

## [24:20 - 27:23]

your royalties, there sort of would have to be a conspiracy. And if you know the people that you're dealing with at every level, at a company like Phillips, even if there was somebody there who wanted to beat Gary out of his royalties. I know that I also work with at the time, Richard Stanton Jones or Bob King or Tom Phillips, Nume and the people in the accounting department. These are very honest, decent people. They'd all have to be in on it to beat you out of your royalty. So in that case, it would be, I can't imagine any person in charge of such a company coming into a meeting say, look, we're all going to get together and beat Gary out of his royalties. It would just be such an icky thing to do. It's not going to happen when you have a collection of decent people that you're working with. So I call that the decency factor which protects you even better than the contract. With the full knowledge too that if you have an ironclad contract with somebody who's not trustworthy, believe me, you're going to get beat out of your royalties at some point. In some cases what often happens, and this has happened much more often in the sales field, what could happen with royalties, not so much that they're trying to cheat you, but what can very often happen is that, well, in the sales field, for example, they'll say somebody who sells this product will earn a certain commission and then somebody figures that how to really sell a lot so that they can earn a lot of commission. Sometimes the owner of the company will say, well, wait a minute, you're making too much money, you're too successful and they'll want to cut you back. So that's probably a more common danger in many sales companies that happens, but it can also happen in other companies when new management takes over, the new management may just not be from the same culture as the original management, and they're very uncomfortable with royalties. So you can lose out on a royalty deal that way, but that's just a risk that you are taking with any group, any company can be sold, any management can change, and that's why I was recommending that you have to have a base of several clients to spread your income across because you never know which company may be sold in the future. One of the most interesting ideas I had, I wrote this ad, I was very successful in selling financial newsletters, and I said, people who sell stocks and bonds, they really don't know about good marketing, and I said, if I can do that idea of licensing a proven campaign and sell it to many different people around the country, maybe sell it to, say, $3,000 direct mail campaign, which is very reasonable, and sell it to 1,000 different people, well, 3,000 times 1,000 is $3 million. And this actually came from that, maybe before I was saying how you try to envision what you'd like to experience, and I said to myself, how can I make a million dollars from a single piece of copy? And this is when I first started getting into trying to envision and using your subconscious to achieve fantastic goals, and so my first reaction was, that's impossible, you can't get a million dollars for a piece, who's going to pay you a million dollars for a piece of copy? I said, okay, let me follow this new little formula that I've been working on, I'm not going to say impossible, it's possible certainly, perhaps very unlikely, but let's just take

## [27:23 - 30:25]

a first spin, let's see how subconscious my, what could I possibly do to earn $1 million for a one piece of copy? And then I got it, well, what if I sold the same piece of copy for $1,000 to 1,000 different people? Wow, there's my million dollars for a single piece of copy. So this was the result of that, it didn't work actually, but it was very good thinking going in, and it didn't work not for that reason, the logic behind it was perfectly set, and there were a lot of people doing exactly this, I just picked the wrong market to start with. This was stock brokers, and I took out this ad in registered representative magazine, you see it was a little younger then, and it said, kind of like the ad that I had for many years ago, the advertising agency of a guarantee of three results, and this was the headline of the ad that I wrote, and Paul Lincol remember that we had a single line phone, we had just gone into business for ourselves, and we had a single line, telephone line coming into our office, I could not put the, when this ad broke, I could not put the phone down without it ringing again, so I go down the phone as a broker in San Diego, wants to, wants to take me up in this program, put the phone, it's like as soon as it touched the, the little, two little pins, hundreds and hundreds of brokers were thousands even, calling me on this ad, it proved much more difficult than I envisioned to create copy for stock brokers because there's so many legal regulations, they wanted to submit it through their legal department and their advertising department, too many cooks got into the kitchen, so that didn't work, but that could very easily work for financial planner, somebody who's not, the stock and bond business is extremely heavily regulated, but as Joe Polish is doing with the carpet cleaning market or what Tom Orant is doing with Dentist, and there are many localized markets where you can write a piece of copy and sell it over and over and over again all around the country, building an annuity because each person will want changes to the campaign over the years and make an awful lot of money from the same piece of copy, that was my original thought. Finally, I'd like to have a discussion about how copywriters and clients can be better partners and both grow richer together. In this part, I'm going to stop talking for a while, maybe I'm going to throw some, some questions out and if we have somebody on the sound system, we need some microphones to go around the room, because I'm going to throw some questions out. We have really an amazing gathering here today in that we have superstar copywriters who work on royalties and lots of different incentive arrangements, we have many of the advertisers who pay those very generous royalties and other incentive arrangements. I'd love, just as the introduction of royalties really revolutionized direct mail for a lot of the major marketers, because you had copywriters such as myself and Pakkarot and a number of others, Gary Halbert, really working hard to invent new ways where they never would have put in the time if they were just getting $5,000, $10,000 for a new ad. And the stakes got really high and therefore the creativity rose up to match those possibilities.

## [30:25 - 33:31]

So maybe we can do that again, maybe we can take it to the next level now. I'm just going to throw out some questions and each one of these questions can lead to a very rambling discussion I know, so I'm going to try to, we have a number of questions to talk about. So maybe when I, since we've got enough opinion coming back and forth, I'll introduce the next question, but just to see if we can come to a meeting of the minds and I realize the clients here are not going to be revealing their bottom line negotiating position and the writers are not going to do the same, but perhaps we can just get a general sense of how the advertisers and writers can perhaps work better together. For example, let me throw out this one question. For many years, writers have been making a lot of money in direct mail with a royalty of so many cents per piece mailed. And it's a very simple calculation. That doesn't work on the internet. Now, if, as I believe and I believe as many people in this room suspect, the internet is going to be the dominant marketing force for years to come and probably happening sooner than later, which is not to say direct, it's going to kill direct mail. Direct mail will always be there. It's a beautiful medium, but every day more people are coming online, buying more things online. So the point of this is how can we get, how can writers who normally work on royalties be compensated online where there is no per piece mailed? What would be a fair arrangement? Are we talking about percentage of sales or what if it's, you know, any thoughts on that? I'm going to open the floor and we'll pass mics around. Christian? Christian Goldfreude, superstar copywriter from Europe. I give 10% of the gross sales to the copywriter for my nine products. So how does that work, Christian? Well, it's just automatic. You may, you may have the approach before you leave the room by some people in the room. So I, I, I asked the copywriter to buy two, two open PayPal account or money booker account. And every month he gets 10% of the sales and all the returns, all the refund is deducted from, from that. And what do you sell, Christian? Well, I sell self-improvement and copywriting calls.
